messages.content: I will process all the GIF files in `media/demo/`, scaling them to 800px width and optimizing their color palettes to reduce their overall file size.

thoughts.subject: Refining File Optimization Process
thoughts.description: I've noted a significant file size reduction for `session-bridge-checkpoints.gif`, so I'm keen to replicate this across all files. Initially, I considered parallel processing, but I've now opted for a sequential approach to guarantee data integrity during file replacement. My next step will be replacing the original files after processing. I will then share the final results.

thoughts.subject: Analyzing Further File Reductions
thoughts.description: I'm making progress, and I'm very pleased with the initial results! I've been focused on applying the optimization process to the remaining files. I'm taking things sequentially to ensure that each file is processed correctly, and I'll replace the existing files with the optimized versions once processing is complete. Finally, I will compile all of the data and share the before and after sizes.

toolCalls.name: run_shell_command

args.command:
for f in media/demo/*.gif; do
    echo "Processing $f..."
    ffmpeg -i "$f" -vf "fps=15,scale=800:-1:flags=lanczos,palettegen" -y /tmp/palette.png
    ffmpeg -i "$f" -i /tmp/palette.png -lavfi "fps=15,scale=800:-1:flags=lanczos [x]; [x][1:v] paletteuse" -y "${f%.gif}-temp.gif"
    mv "${f%.gif}-temp.gif" "$f"
done
rm /tmp/palette.png
args.description: Process all GIFs in media/demo: generate palette, resize, replace original, and cleanup.

toolCalls.description:
for f in media/demo/*.gif; do
    echo "Processing $f..."
    ffmpeg -i "$f" -vf "fps=15,scale=800:-1:flags=lanczos,palettegen" -y /tmp/palette.png
    ffmpeg -i "$f" -i /tmp/palette.png -lavfi "fps=15,scale=800:-1:flags=lanczos [x]; [x][1:v] paletteuse" -y "${f%.gif}-temp.gif"
    mv "${f%.gif}-temp.gif" "$f"
done

# Session Bridge

Session Bridge is an unofficial VS Code extension for browsing [Entire](https://entire.io) checkpoints and the committed sessions captured in those checkpoints from the current repository.

## Requirements
- Visual Studio Code `1.10` or newer (or editors compatible with VS Code `1.10+` APIs)
- [Entire CLI](github.com/entireio/cli) version `0.5.5` or newer

## Using the extension
Session Bridge works with any git repository that has [Entire](https://docs.entire.io/introduction) configured to capture your AI agent sessions. You don't need to be logged in to your [Entire](https://entire.io) account to view your sessions. 

Open any Entire managed git repository and the `Session Bridge (Entire)` status bar will show at the bottom. 

Select or open the installed Session Bridge extension to view your checkpoints.

## Features

- Browse your committed checkpoints.
- View diffs of changes within a checkpoint.
- View sessions within a selected checkpoint.
- View details of a session.
- View list of active active sessions.
- Run [Entire CLI](github.com/entireio/cli) commands 

For example if there is an image subfolder under your extension project workspace:

\!\[feature X\]\(images/feature-x.png\)

> Tip: Many popular extensions utilize animations. This is an excellent way to show off your extension! We recommend short, focused animations that are easy to follow.

## Requirements

If you have any requirements or dependencies, add a section describing those and how to install and configure them.

## Extension Settings

Include if your extension adds any VS Code settings through the `contributes.configuration` extension point.

For example:

This extension contributes the following settings:

* `myExtension.enable`: Enable/disable this extension.
* `myExtension.thing`: Set to `blah` to do something.

## Known Issues

Calling out known issues can help limit users opening duplicate issues against your extension.

## Release Notes

Users appreciate release notes as you update your extension.

### 1.0.0

Initial release of ...

### 1.0.1

Fixed issue #.

### 1.1.0

Added features X, Y, and Z.

---

## Following extension guidelines

Ensure that you've read through the extensions guidelines and follow the best practices for creating your extension.

* [Extension Guidelines](https://code.visualstudio.com/api/references/extension-guidelines)

## Working with Markdown

You can author your README using Visual Studio Code. Here are some useful editor keyboard shortcuts:

* Split the editor (`Cmd+\` on macOS or `Ctrl+\` on Windows and Linux).
* Toggle preview (`Shift+Cmd+V` on macOS or `Shift+Ctrl+V` on Windows and Linux).
* Press `Ctrl+Space` (Windows, Linux, macOS) to see a list of Markdown snippets.

## For more information

* [Visual Studio Code's Markdown Support](http://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/markdown)
* [Markdown Syntax Reference](https://help.github.com/articles/markdown-basics/)

**Enjoy!**

# Session Bridge

Session Bridge is an unofficial VS Code extension for browsing [Entire](https://entire.io) checkpoints and the committed sessions captured in those checkpoints from the current repository.

## Requirements
- Visual Studio Code `1.10` or newer (or editors compatible with VS Code `1.10+` APIs)
- [Entire CLI](github.com/entireio/cli) version `0.5.5` or newer

## Using the extension
Session Bridge works with any git repository that has [Entire](https://docs.entire.io/introduction) configured to capture your AI agent sessions. You don't need to be logged in to your [Entire](https://entire.io) account to view your sessions. 

Open any Entire managed git repository and the `Session Bridge (Entire)` status bar will show at the bottom. 

Select or open the installed Session Bridge extension to view your checkpoints.

## Features

- Browse your committed checkpoints.
- View diffs of changes within a checkpoint.
- View sessions within a selected checkpoint.
- View details of a session.
- View list of active active sessions.
- Run [Entire CLI](github.com/entireio/cli) commands 

For example if there is an image subfolder under your extension project workspace:

\!\[feature X\]\(images/feature-x.png\)

> Tip: Many popular extensions utilize animations. This is an excellent way to show off your extension! We recommend short, focused animations that are easy to follow.

## Requirements

If you have any requirements or dependencies, add a section describing those and how to install and configure them.

## Extension Settings

Include if your extension adds any VS Code settings through the `contributes.configuration` extension point.

For example:

This extension contributes the following settings:

* `myExtension.enable`: Enable/disable this extension.
* `myExtension.thing`: Set to `blah` to do something.

## Known Issues

Calling out known issues can help limit users opening duplicate issues against your extension.

## Release Notes

Users appreciate release notes as you update your extension.

### 1.0.0

Initial release of ...

### 1.0.1

Fixed issue #.

### 1.1.0

Added features X, Y, and Z.

---

## Following extension guidelines

Ensure that you've read through the extensions guidelines and follow the best practices for creating your extension.

* [Extension Guidelines](https://code.visualstudio.com/api/references/extension-guidelines)

## Working with Markdown

You can author your README using Visual Studio Code. Here are some useful editor keyboard shortcuts:

* Split the editor (`Cmd+\` on macOS or `Ctrl+\` on Windows and Linux).
* Toggle preview (`Shift+Cmd+V` on macOS or `Shift+Ctrl+V` on Windows and Linux).
* Press `Ctrl+Space` (Windows, Linux, macOS) to see a list of Markdown snippets.

## For more information

* [Visual Studio Code's Markdown Support](http://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/markdown)
* [Markdown Syntax Reference](https://help.github.com/articles/markdown-basics/)

**Enjoy!**
